rationale: In the citing article, Novemsky et al. (2007) is used as the prior preference-fluency benchmark that managers should make choice formation easier. The authors position their central contribution against this benchmark, arguing and showing that choice effects depend on a mismatch between expected and experienced difficulty, not just absolute ease. They repeatedly use this contrast in framing and managerial implications, indicating a meaningful conceptual role rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper qualifies Novemsky et al. (2007) by arguing that fluency effects in choice depend on discrepancy from expected difficulty. It shows that making decisions harder can sometimes increase premium-option choice when that difficulty shifts fluency expectations, refining the earlier ease-is-better implication.
